1:
The chapter opens on the close of a life and the opening of a reign, and the witnesses read its first sentence — So when David was old and full of days, he made Solomon his son king over Israel — as both an obituary in advance and a constitutional act. The reformed John Gill reckons the king to be now about seventy, perhaps in the last year of his age, and full of days though not yet bedridden. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke lays out the chapter's plan — David makes Solomon king, numbers the Levites and appoints their work, and sets out the houses of Gershom, Kohath, and Merari — and refers the phrase "full of days" back to the death of Abraham in Genesis 25:8.

The grammatical-historical witnesses widen the frame. Keil and Delitzsch treat these four chapters as one connected survey of the Levites toward the end — the fortieth year — of David's reign (cf. 1 Chronicles 26:31): a statement of their total number by the census then taken, their division by their duties (vv.2-5), the heads of the fathers'-houses into which the four Levite families had branched (vv.6-23), and a review of their charge (vv.24-32), before the priestly courses of the next chapter. Charles Ellicott presses the Hebrew: "old and full of days" is literally "old and satisfied with days" (as the verbs stand as adjectives in Genesis 35:29 and Job 42:17), and he hears in it the note of Horace's philosopher departing life like a satisfied guest. He observes too how sparing the Chronicler is: this bare notice that David "made Solomon king" is all he chooses to repeat from the long palace narrative of 1 Kings 1, the family intrigue of Adonijah being no part of his concern.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry draws the doctrine out. The crown is here entailed by divine appointment: David made Solomon king not to reign with him but to reign after him, and did it while old and full of days, satisfied with living, making provision for the welfare of the kingdom after his decease. He did it, Henry notes, "in parliament," in a solemn assembly of all the princes of Israel — which made Adonijah's later attempt to break in upon Solomon's title the more impudent and ridiculous — and he takes it for a great blessing to a people, and a great satisfaction to those leaving the world, when the crown is settled in the interest of the temple. The witnesses do not divide; together they set David at the end of his course, satisfied with days, ordering both throne and sanctuary before he goes.
2:
With the throne secured, the Chronicler shows the king convening a great council, and the witnesses weigh both who was gathered and why. The reformed John Gill takes the assembly as summoned for two ends at once: to make known David's pleasure that Solomon should succeed him, and to settle the order of the temple's work and service. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley reads the same gathering as called to declare God's mind and the king's own will that Solomon be his successor, and to acquaint the leaders with the directions David had received from God "by the spirit."

The grammatical-historical witnesses fix the constitutional weight of the moment. Keil and Delitzsch note that David collected "all the princes of Israel, with the priests and the Levites," the princes because the numbering of the Levites and the ordering of their duties was a matter of national importance; in a solemn assembly of the representatives of the lay tribes, together with priests and Levites, the king fixed the arrangements the chapter records. Charles Ellicott presses the grammar: the form of the verb, the imperfect with waw conversive, implies that this convening was bound up with the transfer of the kingdom to Solomon, so that all the following chapters belong to the arrangements David made toward the close of his life — a dating he anchors to the "fortieth year of the reign of David" at 1 Chronicles 26:31. The princes, he adds, comparing 1 Chronicles 13:1, 1 Chronicles 15:25, and 1 Chronicles 22:17, together with the priests and the Levites, constituted in the Chronicler's conception the three estates of the realm — the representatives of all spiritual and temporal authority — so that in a matter of national concern David consults the whole national assembly rather than acting alone.

The witnesses do not contend. They agree that this is no private household arrangement but a public, deliberate settlement: the aged king, before his death, gathers the responsible heads of the nation and its clergy and, in their sight, both names his heir and orders the service of the house that heir is to build. Gill's twofold purpose — the succession and the temple — is exactly the double business the assembly is called to transact, and Ellicott's three estates give the scene its formal dignity. The reader is meant to see that the reorganization of the Levites which follows carries the authority not of one man's whim but of the assembled realm, and that it was undertaken in the same breath as the securing of Solomon's crown.
3:
The census reaches its total here — their number by their polls, man by man, was thirty and eight thousand — and the witnesses at once meet the chronological knot that will run through the chapter. The reformed John Gill notes that the Levites were numbered "from thirty years old and upward," as they had first been in the days of Moses, though a new reckoning would follow at v.24. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch confirm that this agrees with Numbers 4:3, where Moses numbered those from thirty to fifty for the service of the tabernacle in the wilderness; yet Moses himself later fixed the term of service from twenty-five to fifty (Numbers 8:24), and since v.24 of this very chapter states plainly that the Levites were numbered from twenty years old, and since the practice from Hezekiah onward reckoned from twenty (2 Chronicles 31:17; Ezra 3:8), they judge that David did not in fact confine the count to those of thirty and upward. Charles Ellicott describes it as a census like that Moses instituted (Numbers 4:3), of all Levites from thirty to fifty for the work of the tabernacle, and glosses "by their polls, man by man" as literally "as to their skulls, as to men" — the second phrase defining the first and excluding women and children from the tally.

The wesleyan-arminian voices supply the reason the upper limit has here fallen away. Adam Clarke observes that the fifty-year limit of Numbers 4:3 is not mentioned now, probably because the service was no longer so laborious: the ark being fixed, the Levites had no heavy burdens to carry, so that even an older man might continue to serve — and he points forward to the fresh ordinance David would make (v.24). John Wesley reasons the same way from the other end. The old rule ran only to fifty because the Levites had to carry the tabernacle and its vessels from place to place, and God would free them from that toil when age came on; but that reason wholly ceased once the temple was to be built, the work grown far easier and the service now more a privilege than a burden, so their time was justly prolonged toward death.

So already at the total the witnesses have sighted the tension the chapter will resolve — thirty here, twenty at v.24 — and have begun to reconcile it by the changed nature of the Levites' work once the ark had rest. They agree that the figure of thirty-eight thousand marks not a mere muster but the vast increase of Levi's tribe, now to be set in order for a settled house of worship.
4:
Now the great body of the Levites is parcelled out by function, and the witnesses note first that these are David's own words. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott and Keil and Delitzsch agree that the king himself is speaking here and through the next verse, personally assigning the Levites their commission — Keil supplying an unspoken "and David said" before the verse, and Ellicott rendering the peculiar Hebrew, "Of these let there be for superintending the work of the house of Jehovah twenty-four thousand, and scribes and judges six thousand." The verb "to set forward," Ellicott adds, is the very word whose participle heads so many psalms as "chief musician"; it means to lead or superintend. The reformed John Gill fills in what that superintending work was: the twenty-four thousand were to prepare for the priests by slaying the sacrifices, flaying, cutting, and washing them, and bringing them to the altar, a thousand of them serving in weekly turns.

On the six thousand "officers and judges" the witnesses divide the ground between them. Ellicott explains "officers" (shoterim), first met at Exodus 5:6 and again at Deuteronomy 16:18, as literally "writers," and sees in the whole arrangement the elevation of the Levites, in Ewald's phrase, from a warlike troop of the sanctuary's defenders into peaceful guardians of the temple and its treasures, musicians, instructors, and judges scattered through the land. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley sharpens the distinction: the officers were to see that all the temple work about the sacrifices was punctually performed, by courses of a thousand at a time, while the judges served "not in the affairs of the temple" — there the priests presided — but in the several parts of the kingdom, assisting the princes and elders of every tribe in the administration of justice.

Ellicott presses one further detail that keeps the picture honest: the more menial labour of the sanctuary need not all have fallen on the Levites themselves, for they had a share in the prisoners of war (Numbers 31:30), and the Gibeonites had been spared on condition of becoming hewers of wood and drawers of water — Levitical bondsmen — as whole cities may have been (Joshua 9:23). The witnesses converge on the shape of the reform: out of the thirty-eight thousand, the largest company by far is set to the daily service of the house, a smaller body sent out as magistrates over the land, and the whole tribe turned from its old task of carriage to the settled offices of a fixed and central worship.
5:
The last two companies are named — four thousand were porters; and four thousand praised the LORD with the instruments which I made — and here, over the music, the witnesses genuinely part. The reformed John Gill sets the scene plainly: four thousand porters kept the east, north, and south gates in their turns. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott dwells on the closing clause, "the instruments that I have made for praising," as proof that this whole verse and the last are David's own spoken decree, set as it were within quotation marks; and he finds in it an arresting fact — that David was not only minstrel and inspired psalmist but an inventor of stringed instruments. He hears the same tradition behind the prophet's complaint of the nobles "that invent instruments of music, like David" at Amos 6:5, and behind the reference at Nehemiah 12:36; Ewald even took the clause for a fragment of an old poet speaking in the name of Jehovah, of those "whom I have formed to sing my praise."

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke reads that very same cross-reference against the grain. David, he grants, made this distribution out of his own judgment and piety — "but it does not appear that he had any positive Divine authority for such arrangements"; and as for the instruments, he takes them to be condemned elsewhere, pointing to Amos 6:5 as a passage this verse is allowed to parallel, where the instruments "like David" are named in a word of rebuke. So a real difference stands: the same verse of Amos that Ellicott hears as a witness to David's honoured inventiveness, Clarke hears as a prophetic reproach, and where Ellicott sees a divinely gifted institution of temple song, Clarke sees a human arrangement without express warrant.

Keil and Delitzsch keep to the structure, joining this verse to the next by noting how David "divided them into courses" among the sons of Levi, and observing that these two bodies — the four thousand porters and the four thousand singers — stand apart from the twenty-four thousand of the general service and from the officers and judges, each company with its own office. The witnesses thus leave the reader with the full fourfold division complete — servers, magistrates, gatekeepers, and singers — and with an honest disagreement running through the last of them: whether the sacred music David here founded was the Spirit's own provision for the house of God or a pious innovation of the king's that a later prophet would find occasion to censure.
6:
With the numbers assigned, the record turns to the division by descent — David divided them into courses among the sons of Levi, Gershon, and Kohath, and Merari — and the witnesses read the verse as the doorway into the long genealogical roster that follows. The reformed John Gill takes it simply: David sorted the Levites into three classes according to their number, answering to the three sons of Levi about to be named. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott is more exact about who was divided. "Among the sons of Levi," he urges, is better "according to the sons of Levi" — that is, according to the three great subdivisions of the tribe (1 Chronicles 6:1) — and the "them" who are divided are the twenty-four thousand superintendents of v.4, not the whole tribe. He notes the reflexive Hebrew form, "he divided him them," comparing 1 Chronicles 24:3, and points out the correct spelling here, "Gershon," not the "Gershom" found elsewhere.

Ellicott adds an observation that governs the reader's expectation for the whole chapter: many of the names about to be enumerated recur in the lists of 1 Chronicles 24:20 and 1 Chronicles 26:20, whereas the courses of the musicians (1 Chronicles 25:1), the warders (1 Chronicles 26:1), and the scribes and judges (1 Chronicles 26:29) are altogether different sets of names. The roster that follows, in other words, belongs specifically to the twenty-four thousand assigned to the service of the house, not to the gatekeepers or singers already counted.

Keil and Delitzsch attend to the same verb and its disputed pointing, weighing whether it should be read as the simple, the reflexive, or the intensive voice — a question, they conclude with Kimchi and the more accurate manuscripts, of the vowel-points only, the consonants remaining the same. But the substance is not in doubt. The witnesses agree that the verse announces the plan of everything from here to the chapter's genealogical close: the Levites of the general service are to be arrayed under the three ancestral houses of Gershon, Kohath, and Merari, and it is the branching of those three houses into their fathers'-families — nine of Gershon and Kohath each, six of Merari — that the following verses will patiently trace, until the four-and-twenty courses answering to the four-and-twenty thousand are complete.
7:
The genealogy of the Gershonites begins — of the Gershonites were Laadan and Shimei — and the witnesses open a small question of identity over the first name. The reformed John Gill takes Laadan and Shimei for the immediate posterity of Gershon, and identifies them with the Libni and Shimhi named as Gershon's sons elsewhere, heads of the two Gershonite families. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott is more cautious: comparing 1 Chronicles 6:1, where the two principal branches of the Gershonites are called Libni and Shimei, he judges that "Laadan is hardly the same as Libni," but rather a branch that had become prominent in David's own day — so that the roster reflects the living clans of the tenth century, not simply the names of the patriarchal sons.

Ellicott sets out the architecture of the section. Verses seven through eleven give the names of nine Gershonite houses, or guilds; David's courses were formed according to the natural divisions already existing among the Levites, coinciding with the fathers'-houses, and were doubtless twenty-four in number, like the courses of their brethren the musicians (1 Chronicles 25:31) and like the priestly classes (1 Chronicles 24:4) — a total Josephus himself records.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch, before entering the list, pause on the very idea of a fathers'-house. In the natural growth of Israel, they explain, the twelve sons of Jacob founded the tribes, his grandsons the families, and their sons the fathers'-houses; but this tidy scheme was never consistently carried through, for some descendants grew too numerous to remain single families and others too few to stand alone, so that houses were joined or subdivided as the generations required. That principle, they note, is exactly what the following verses will display among the sons of Gershon, where two ancestral names branch into nine actual houses. The witnesses thus prepare the reader to read the roster not as a rigid family tree but as a register of the working divisions of the Levites as they stood when David numbered them, the ancient names of Gershon's sons overlaid by the clans that bore rule in the service of the house in the king's last days.
8:
The house of Laadan is now opened — the sons of Laadan; the chief was Jehiel, and Zetham, and Joel, three — and the witnesses read the three names with an eye to the tangle just ahead. The reformed John Gill enters a caution at once: these were not Laadan's immediate sons but some who descended from him, the "chief" Jehiel standing at the head of a house rather than being a child of Laadan's own body. The point matters, for the register throughout names the heads of families as they stood in David's day, often several generations below the ancestor whose name the house still bears.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott lays out the arrangement that makes the passage confusing. The sons of Laadan are named in two groups: the three of this verse, and then three more in the next, who are there called "sons of Shimei." But that Shimei, he warns, is not the Shimei of v.7, Laadan's brother, whose own subdivisions are not given until v.10; he is rather a descendant within Laadan's line, so that six houses in all are reckoned to Laadan — the three here and the three of v.9. Keil and Delitzsch confirm the count, listing the sons of this Laadan as Jehiel, Zetham, and Joel, three, and then the sons of the second Shimei as Shelomith, Haziel, and Haran, three, and summing them as "the chief of the fathers of Laadan."

The witnesses agree, then, that the reader must hold two men named Shimei apart — the son of Gershon and brother of Laadan on the one hand, and a later Shimei reckoned among Laadan's descendants on the other — or the six houses of Laadan will be hopelessly confused with the three of the elder Shimei. It is a characteristic difficulty of these Levitical rolls, where the same honoured names recur in different generations, and where the compiler counts by working houses rather than by simple descent; and the witnesses' whole labour on the verse is to keep the branches distinct, so that the nine Gershonite houses may be told out correctly when the list is done.
9:
The second group of Laadan's descendants is named — the sons of Shimei; Shelomith, and Haziel, and Haran, three. These were the chief of the fathers of Laadan — and the witnesses use the closing clause to total the elder Gershonite branch. The reformed John Gill first settles which Shimei is meant: not the son of Gershon, Laadan's brother, but a descendant of Laadan himself, as Kimchi observes — the same second Shimei the previous verse had introduced. So these three, Shelomith, Haziel, and Haran, belong under Laadan, not under the brother Shimei whose own sons are still to come.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott takes the summing clause carefully. "These were the chief of the fathers of Laadan" he would render rather "heads of the fathers'-houses belonging to Laadan"; and the names, he suggests, are at once the names of the clans and of their living chiefs — the man and the house he heads bearing the one designation. Adding this trio to the three of v.8, he reckons that to Laadan pertained six houses in all: Jehiel, Zetham, and Joel, then Shelomith, Haziel, and Haran.

The witnesses find in this quiet arithmetic the Chronicler's real concern. He is not tracing a bloodline for its own sake but counting the working divisions of the Gershonite service, and the verse exists to close the tally of Laadan's houses before the record turns, in the next verses, to the houses of the elder Shimei. That the same name — Shimei — should stand both for Gershon's second son and for a descendant within Laadan's line is precisely the sort of overlap that makes these lists hard; but the witnesses, by fixing the six houses of Laadan here, clear the ground for the three of Shimei that follow, so that the nine Gershonite fathers'-houses will stand out plainly. Gill's identification and Ellicott's rendering pull together to the same end: to keep the elder branch of Gershon distinct and fully numbered, six houses strong, each with its head, as they were mustered for the service of the house of the LORD in the last year of David.
10:
The record turns from Laadan to the elder Shimei — the sons of Shimei were Jahath, Zina, and Jeush, and Beriah; these four were the sons of Shimei — and the witnesses first make sure the right man is in view. The reformed John Gill identifies him as the son of Gershon and brother of Laadan, following Kimchi, which he thinks the correct reading; so the four names here spring from the second great Gershonite stem, not from Laadan's line just completed.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott counts the houses and catches a curiosity of spelling. The sons of this Shimei formed four houses, yet were reckoned as only three, because the two last named, Jeush and Beriah, were numerically weak and so were counted together as a single house and class — a point the next verse will state. And he notes that "Zina" here is spelt "Zizah" one verse later, the same name appearing quite differently in two consecutive verses; "Zizah" is probably the right form, supported by the Septuagint and Vulgate, while the Syriac and Arabic read "Zabda." Keil and Delitzsch agree on the reckoning: from the four sons of Shimei — Jahath, Zina, Jeush, and Beriah — three fathers'-houses were derived, the last two combined into one, so that the Gershonites of David's day numbered nine houses in all, six named after Laadan and three after this Shimei.

The witnesses do not divide over the substance; they only guard the reader against two confusions the verse invites — the confusion of this Shimei with the later Shimei of Laadan's line, and the confusion of "Zina" with "Zizah," a single house wearing two spellings. What they draw out is the same principle that has shaped the whole Gershonite roster: the compiler numbers by working houses, not by heads counted one for one, so that four sons can yield but three houses when two of them are too few to stand alone. It is a small instance of the honest realism of these lists, which record the tribe not as an ideal genealogy but as it actually stood, some branches multiplied and some so diminished that they had to be joined to keep the service manned.
11:
The inner ordering of Shimei's house is given — Jahath was the chief, and Zizah the second; but Jeush and Beriah had not many sons, therefore they were in one reckoning, according to their father's house — and the witnesses fasten on the combining of the two smaller families. The reformed John Gill notes only that Jahath was the chief, the prince and head of a family, standing first among the sons of Shimei. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke draws out the reason for the joining: the smaller family, having few sons, was united with another so that the two together might do the work which properly belonged to one house but would have been too much for either of them standing separately. The union, in other words, was practical — a matter of manning a fixed share of the service — not a mere note of kinship.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott states the arithmetic that results. Because Jeush and Beriah "had not many sons," they became one fathers'-house, one "muster" or class; and so, adding the six houses of Laadan to the three of Shimei, there were nine Gershonite clans in all among the twenty-four thousand Levites of v.4. Here the spelling settles too: the "Zina" of the previous verse stands as "Zizah" the second, the same man under the form Ellicott judged the true one.

The witnesses converge on the quiet logic of the whole Gershonite section. A house existed to furnish its thousand for the service; where a branch had grown too thin to furnish its share, it was folded into another, so that the roll of working houses might stay even and the courses of the temple never fall short of hands. Clarke's observation gives the theological colour the bare names withhold: even in so administrative a matter as counting Levites, the weak were not simply dropped but joined to the strong, that together they might bear a burden neither could carry alone — the little and the large set side by side, and made, in the service of God's house, to count as one. So the elder Shimei's four sons close as three houses, and the nine houses of Gershon stand complete, ready for the record to pass on to the sons of Kohath.
12:
The roster crosses from the first of Levi's sons to the second — the sons of Kohath; Amram, Izhar, Hebron, and Uzziel, four — and the witnesses mark the fresh branch and its dignity. The reformed John Gill notes simply that Kohath was the second son of Levi, his four sons here beginning the central Levitical stem. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott sets out the span of the section: verses twelve through twenty give the names of nine Kohathite houses, opening with Amram and Izhar as at 1 Chronicles 6:1, so that Kohath, like Gershon before him, will branch into nine fathers'-houses.

Keil and Delitzsch weigh the peculiar weight of this family. The four sons of Kohath — Amram, Izhar, Hebron, and Uzziel — founded the four Kohathite families known already from Numbers 3:27; and from the eldest, Amram, came Aaron and Moses. That descent lifts the house of Kohath above its brethren, for from it sprang both the priesthood and the great prophet-lawgiver; and the witnesses will find, as the list unfolds, that the Chronicler must here carefully divide the one line that rose to the altar from the other that remained among the common Levites. Aaron and his sons, set apart to the priesthood, will be lifted out of the reckoning of the fathers'-houses, while Moses' own posterity, as the coming verses insist, are numbered as ordinary Levites and no more.

For the present the witnesses are content to name the four founders and note the house's coming eminence. The verse does for Kohath what v.7 did for Gershon: it plants the ancestral names from which the actual houses of David's day will be drawn, and it prepares the reader for the delicate business of the next verses, where the single family of Amram divides into the priestly line of Aaron and the Levitical line of Moses. The tribe's holiest blood runs through this second son of Levi, and the Chronicler, for all his interest in the throne of David, pauses to trace with equal and unhurried care the very house from which both the ministers and the great lawgiver of the sanctuary came.
13:
Here the central family divides, and the witnesses gather around the exaltation of Aaron. The sons of Amram; Aaron and Moses: and Aaron was separated, that he should sanctify the most holy things, he and his sons for ever. The reformed John Gill marks the bare fact — Aaron and Moses were the sons of Amram, a son of Kohath — while the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott explains why Aaron is named only to be set aside: he and his sons, as priests, are excluded from the present reckoning of Levitical houses and form the proper subject of the next chapter; they appear here merely for completeness. Ellicott presses the Hebrew of "sanctify the most holy things," rendering it rather "to hallow him as most holy" — literally "holy of holies," an expression, he notes, applied to Aaron in no other passage of Scripture, marking the priesthood as a higher grade of consecration than the mere Levites because called to a holier ministry. "To burn incense," he adds, is a phrase that covers the burning of victims as well as incense, and "to minister unto him and to bless in his name" are the very words used of the tribe's separation at Deuteronomy 10:8, the blessing itself being that of Numbers 6:23.

Keil and Delitzsch agree that Aaron was set apart to be a most holy one, he and his sons for ever, to offer incense before the LORD, to serve him, and to bless in his name — and they too reject the older glosses that would make the phrase mean ministering in the holy of holies, or consecrating the sacred vessels. The wesleyan-arminian voices add the pastoral sense. Adam Clarke takes "to bless in his name" as blessing the people by invoking the name of the Lord; John Wesley reads "sanctify" as keeping the most holy things from pollution, since they were profaned if any but a priest touched them, and takes "he and his sons" to reach not only the successive high priests but all the priests, the offices here named being common to them all.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry, surveying the chapter, sets the two brothers in a moral light the bare list does not. It is a mark of Moses' self-denial that his own posterity stood level with the common Levites, while Aaron's was advanced to the priesthood; and the elevation of Aaron's house above his brother's was itself a recompense for the meekness with which Aaron had once submitted to his younger brother's leadership. Those content to stoop, Henry observes, are in the fairest way to rise. The witnesses do not divide; they let the verse do its double work — parting the priestly line from the Levitical within the one house of Amram, and setting Aaron's consecration, most holy, at the summit of the whole tribe.
14:
The other son of Amram is now taken up, and with a striking restraint. Now concerning Moses the man of God, his sons were named of the tribe of Levi — that is, his sons were reckoned among the common Levites, not among the priests. The reformed John Gill pauses on the honorific: Moses is "the man of God," raised up as a prophet, admitted to great familiarity with God, a lawgiver from him, and the ruler and guide of Israel through the wilderness. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott explains the force of the phrase "named of the tribe of Levi," comparing Genesis 48:6: Aaron's sons were priests, but the sons of Moses were counted simple Levites, and so their houses are enumerated among the ordinary Levitical families. The title "man of God," he adds, is one charged with a divine mission — used of Moses at Deuteronomy 33:1, borne by the prophets under the kings, given even to David (Nehemiah 12:24), and applied at last by Paul to Timothy (1 Timothy 6:11).

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley finds in the very lowliness of Moses' sons a monument to their father. That they were accounted only common Levites, and not priests, is set down "for the honour of Moses, and the demonstration of his eminent piety and self-denial," who willingly left the government to Joshua and the priesthood to Aaron, and was content to see his own posterity reduced to a private and mean condition. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry, surveying the chapter, presses the same wonder: the posterity of Moses, that great man, stood upon the level with common Levites and had no dignities or privileges peculiar to them — a proof that he was no self-seeker, leaving his children no marks of distinction, a sign that he had the spirit of God and not the spirit of the world; for had he aimed to raise his own family he might easily have done so.

The witnesses do not contend. They agree that the verse quietly honours Moses by recording how little he sought for his own. The lawgiver who might have founded a priestly dynasty founds nothing; his sons melt into the ranks of Levi, indistinguishable from the rest, and Scripture notes it not as a slight but as the crown of his humility. The greatness of the man is measured, the witnesses say, precisely by the obscurity he was willing to leave his house — the meekest of men content that his name should carry no earthly privilege into the generations that came after him.
15:
The two sons of Moses are named — the sons of Moses were Gershom and Eliezer — and the witnesses trace how each becomes the head of a Levitical house. The reformed John Gill notes only the pair, referring the reader to the earlier record of their birth. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch develop the descent: each of the two sons founded a fathers'-house, Gershom through his son Shebuel and Eliezer through Rehabiah; and though only the one head is named in each case, the plural "sons" is used, either because there were other sons reckoned into the same house or because the named son had sons of his own who together made up the house. From the remark of v.17 — that Eliezer had no son but Rehabiah, while Rehabiah's sons were very many — they infer that Gershom, by contrast, must have had other sons besides Shebuel, whose descendants were simply numbered under Shebuel's house.

Charles Ellicott attends to the names themselves. "Gershom," he explains, means "expulsion" and is a variant form of Gershon, the account in Exodus 2:22 giving an allusive play on the name rather than a strict derivation; "Eliezer" means "God is help," and is a distinct name from Eleazar, "God hath helped," with which it is easily confused. Both names, he notes, are given at Exodus 18:4, where their meaning is unfolded in Moses' own words.

The witnesses agree on the modest but real point of the verse: the sons of the great lawgiver take their place as founders of two ordinary Levitical houses, Shebuel's and Rehabiah's, no different in standing from the houses of Gershon and Merari. The line of Moses is not exalted but simply continued, branching like any other into its fathers'-houses; and the care with which Keil and Delitzsch reason out the hidden sons of Gershom, and Ellicott the sense of the names, shows how the Chronicler means even this most honoured descent to be read as one strand among many in the working roster of Levi — the prophet's grandsons manning the service of the house alongside their kinsmen, their genealogy preserved not for glory but for order.
16:
A single name carries the house of Gershom — of the sons of Gershom, Shebuel was the chief — and the witnesses gather what tradition attached to it. The reformed John Gill records that this Shebuel, of the posterity of Gershom in David's day, is called Shubael elsewhere, and passes on the Targum's identification of him with Jonathan. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke gives that legend in fuller form: the Targum adds that this same Jonathan, who had become a false prophet, repented in his old age, and that David made him his chief treasurer — a rabbinic gloss binding the obscure name to a story of apostasy and recovery.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott reads the verse more soberly for what its wording implies. That "Shebuel was the chief," or head, of the sons of Gershom implies that Gershom had other sons not named here, who were reckoned members of the one house that bore Shebuel's headship; and he confirms that Shebuel appears as Shubael in the parallel list of 1 Chronicles 24:20, the same man carried under two forms of the one name. Keil and Delitzsch, tracing the same line, take Shebuel as the head through whom Gershom's fathers'-house was constituted, the other descendants gathered silently under him.

The witnesses thus hold together the plain sense and the legend. On the surface the verse merely names the chief of Gershom's house; beneath it the Jewish tradition, preserved by Gill and Clarke, could not resist reading the grandson of Moses through the darker history of a Jonathan who fell into false prophecy and was afterward restored. Whether or not the identification holds, the witnesses let it stand as a reminder of how these bare names drew stories to themselves in later memory. What Ellicott and Keil and Delitzsch insist on is the structural truth the name conveys: one man stands as head, and a whole unnamed company of Gershom's posterity is numbered in him, so that the house of the prophet's elder son is entered on the roll under a single representative chief, as the compiler's method requires throughout these lists, the many reckoned in the one.
17:
The house of Moses' second son turns on a striking contrast — the sons of Eliezer were, Rehabiah the chief; and Eliezer had none other sons, but the sons of Rehabiah were very many. The reformed John Gill notes that Rehabiah was the first and only begotten of Eliezer, so that a single son carried the whole line forward. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott sharpens the wording: Rehabiah is "the chief," the founder and eponym of the clan called after him, and the Hebrew says literally that "there became not to Eliezer other sons, and the sons of Rehabiah had multiplied exceedingly" — grown, he renders, "unto height," the very phrase used at 1 Chronicles 22:5. The clan of Rehabiah, in short, was very populous, so that the descendants of Moses were comprised in just two fathers'-houses, Shebuel's and Rehabiah's, the one slender line suddenly broadening into a multitude.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke passes on the Targum's extravagant measure of that increase: "on account of the merits of Moses, the posterity of Rehabiah were multiplied to more than sixty myriads" — a number set deliberately to echo the whole host of Israel at the Exodus. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry, surveying the chapter, draws the same multiplication into a lesson of providence. When God had proposed to Moses that, if he would let fall his intercession for Israel, he would make of him a great nation, Moses generously refused; and here, Henry says, his family is recompensed for that refusal, greatly increased, making up in number what it wanted in dignity, so that the house which sought no greatness for itself is filled out in the tribe of Levi.

The witnesses converge on the quiet justice of it. Eliezer has but one son, and that son's house swells beyond all the rest — a fruitfulness the tradition and the puritan alike read as heaven's answer to Moses' self-denial. The man who would not be made a great nation at Israel's expense is given, without his seeking, a numerous seed within the ministering tribe; and the Chronicler, by pausing to note that Rehabiah's sons "were very many," preserves the memory of a house that grew great precisely because its father had been willing to remain small.
18:
The record returns from Moses' line to the wider house of Kohath — of the sons of Izhar; Shelomith the chief — and the witnesses note how a whole branch is gathered into one name. The reformed John Gill identifies Izhar as another son of Kohath, the son of Levi, his posterity now taken up after the digression through Amram's sons Aaron and Moses. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott specifies that Izhar, Kohath's second son, made but a single clan, that of Shelomith — spelt Shelomoth in the parallel of 1 Chronicles 24:22 — and observes that this is the same variation of form already met with in the Hebrew at v.9, where a name wavered between two spellings from one verse to the next.

Keil and Delitzsch confirm the reckoning: only one son of Izhar, the brother of Amram, is mentioned, Shelomith standing as head, after whom the whole Izharite fathers'-house was named. So where Gershom's line and Eliezer's each yielded a house, Izhar's yields but one as well, the single Shelomith representing the entire branch before the record passes on; the other descendants of Izhar, if there were any, are reckoned silently under his headship, as the compiler's manner throughout has been to name the chief alone and gather the rest into him.

The witnesses find nothing to dispute and much to keep straight. The verse is one more instance of the compiler's steady method: a founder's name, a single head, and a house numbered in him — the Izharites entered on the roll under Shelomith as the Gershomites were entered under Shebuel. What small interest the verse holds lies in the wavering of the name, Shelomith or Shelomoth, which Ellicott ties back to the like wavering at v.9, a reminder that these rolls were copied and recopied until a single man might stand in the record under two forms of his name. The witnesses' care to identify Izhar rightly, and to fix his one house, keeps the count of the nine Kohathite fathers'-houses moving toward its total: Amram's line divided into priest and Levite, and now Izhar's gathered whole into Shelomith, with Hebron's and Uzziel's houses still to come before Kohath's nine are complete.
19:
The third son of Kohath brings a fuller branch — of the sons of Hebron; Jeriah the first, Amariah the second, Jahaziel the third, and Jekameam the fourth — and the witnesses count the houses it yields. The reformed John Gill marks Hebron simply as a third son of Kohath, his four sons now named in order. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott observes that the little word "of" is wanting in the Hebrew here, as it is at v.16, v.18, and v.20, the text running abruptly from name to name; and he reckons the sons of Hebron as four houses, clans, or classes, whose names recur in the parallel muster of 1 Chronicles 24:23.

Keil and Delitzsch set the branch in the sum of the whole Kohathite reckoning. Amram's next brother Hebron had four sons, and the youngest brother, Uzziel, two, who founded fathers'-houses; so that, besides the priests drawn off from Amram's line, nine Levitical fathers'-houses descended from Kohath, and their chiefs who served in the sanctuary are enumerated again at 1 Chronicles 24:20 and following. Hebron's four thus stand as the largest single contribution to the Kohathite total, ranged first, second, third, and fourth in a fixed order of precedence, each in its turn a head of house among the twenty-four courses that were to serve the sanctuary.

The witnesses do not divide; the verse is plain, and their work is chiefly to keep the tally and to notice the ranking. That the sons are numbered "the first... the fourth" shows the compiler's concern for order of standing among the houses, each with its place in the rota of service; and Ellicott's small grammatical note — the missing "of" — reminds the reader how terse and list-like the Hebrew has become, the narrative voice fallen silent as pure register takes over. With Hebron's four houses added to Amram's Levitical remnant and Izhar's one, only Uzziel's two remain to complete the nine houses of Kohath, and the witnesses carry the count steadily forward to the next verse, where the youngest son's branch will at last close the Kohathite roll.
20:
The youngest of Kohath's sons closes the branch — of the sons of Uzziel; Michah the first, and Jesiah the second — and with only two witnesses at hand the reading is brief but exact. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott states that the sons of Uzziel constituted two houses and classes, Michah and Jesiah, ranked first and second as Hebron's four had been ranked; and he notes that the nine clans of Kohathite Levites are again rehearsed together in the parallel list of 1 Chronicles 24:20 and following, where the same houses reappear with their serving chiefs.

Keil and Delitzsch, gathering the whole reckoning of Kohath, confirm the sum: Amram's next brother Hebron had four sons and the youngest brother Uzziel two, all founders of fathers'-houses, so that — the priests of Aaron's line drawn off and Moses' sons numbered among the Levites — nine Levitical fathers'-houses in all descended from Kohath. Uzziel's two, Michah and Jesiah, are the last of them, and with their naming the Kohathite roll stands complete: Amram's Levitical portion, Izhar's single house of Shelomith, Hebron's four, and now Uzziel's two.

The witnesses find no dispute in so plain a verse, and their labour is simply to close the count and to bind it to the fuller catalogue of the next chapter. That the same nine houses are set down twice — here in the numbering under David, and again at 1 Chronicles 24:20, where their chiefs are appointed by lot alongside the priests — is itself, to the grammatical-historical eye, a mark of the record's coherence: the working divisions counted in this chapter are the very divisions ordered for service in that one. So Kohath, the house from which both the priesthood and the lawgiver had come, is shown branching in David's day into nine ministering families, no more exalted in the roster than Gershon's nine, each entered under its head and ranked for its turn in the service of the house of God. With Uzziel's two the second great stem of Levi is told out, and the record is ready to pass to the sons of Merari.
21:
The last of Levi's three stems is opened — the sons of Merari; Mahli, and Mushi. The sons of Mahli; Eleazar, and Kish — and the two grammatical-historical witnesses trace both the branch and the law that will shape it. Charles Ellicott anchors the names in the older registers, referring the sons of Merari, Mahli and Mushi, to Exodus 6:19, Numbers 3:33, and 1 Chronicles 6:19, where the same two stand as the founders of the Merarite families. Keil and Delitzsch develop the descent: as in those passages, two sons of Merari — Mahli and Mushi — founded the two Merarite families that existed already in the time of Moses.

Then Keil and Delitzsch follow the line of Mahli into a difficulty the next verse will name outright. Mahli had two sons, Eleazar and Kish; but Eleazar, they note, left at his death only daughters, who were married to the sons of Kish — their cousins — according to the law governing daughters who were heiresses (Numbers 36:6). The descendants of Mahli were therefore not carried on under Eleazar's name but comprehended in the single fathers'-house of Kish, whose head at that time, as the parallel record shows, was Jerahmeel (1 Chronicles 24:29). So the branch that began with two sons narrows at once toward one, Eleazar's line surviving only by marriage into Kish's house.

The witnesses, both of the grammatical-historical school, read the verse as the setting of a small drama of inheritance. The Merarites, fewest of the three great houses, will yield the last of the twenty-four courses; and already in this opening verse the compiler has planted the circumstance — Eleazar's daughters, the heiress law, the absorption into Kish — that keeps the Merarite count from breaking the pattern. What in Gershon and Kohath was managed by joining small houses is here managed by the marriage of heiresses, the same practical end reached by another means. The witnesses thus enter the Merarite stem with the machinery of its reckoning already in view, Mahli and Mushi its two founders, and Mahli's own line poised to run, through daughters, into the house of his son Kish.
22:
The circumstance sketched a verse before is now stated outright — Eleazar died, and had no sons, but daughters; and their brethren the sons of Kish took them — and the witnesses read it as a small case of the law of heiresses. The reformed John Gill notes the plain want: Eleazar died leaving no sons but daughters, and so none to succeed him in his office and service. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke supplies the legal ground for what followed: the sons of Kish took Eleazar's daughters in marriage according to the statute made for the daughters of Zelophehad (Numbers 27:1; Numbers 36:5), that women who were heiresses should marry within the family of their father's tribe, so that their estates should not be alienated from it.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott draws the genealogical consequence: because Eleazar died sonless, his house merged in that of the sons of Kish, who married his daughters according to the law (Numbers 36:6); and so the line of Mahli was represented in David's day by the single house of Kish (1 Chronicles 24:29). Keil and Delitzsch reach the same result — Mahli's descendants comprehended in the one fathers'-house of Kish, whose head was Jerahmeel — and read the whole arrangement as the reason the Merarites, though springing from two sons, could still be numbered cleanly among the twenty-four courses.

The witnesses agree, without contention, on the substance: a Levitical inheritance, threatened with lapse when a man died leaving only daughters, was preserved by their marriage to their cousins, the sons of Kish, so that name and office and portion stayed within the house of Merari. Clarke's appeal to Zelophehad's daughters gives the verse its warrant in the Mosaic law; Ellicott and Keil and Delitzsch trace its effect through the register, where Eleazar's line survives not under his own name but folded into Kish's. It is one more instance of the quiet exactness with which the Chronicler manages his roll: even a house that failed of sons is not lost from the count, but carried forward through its daughters, that the full number of the Merarite fathers'-houses might stand when the tale of the courses came to be told.
23:
The Merarite roll closes with the sons of Mushi — Mahli, and Eder, and Jeremoth, three — and here the witnesses meet the hardest textual knot of the chapter, for the houses will not add up. The reformed John Gill passes over the difficulty, noting only that Mushi was the other son of Merari, his three sons now named. But the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott spreads the problem open. These three, with the one house of Kish, make only four Merarite houses, whereas six are needed to bring the Levitical total to twenty-four — nine Gershonite, nine Kohathite, and six Merarite, at a thousand to the course for the twenty-four thousand of v.4. The parallel at 1 Chronicles 24:26 names a third son of Merari, Jaaziah, whose descendants formed three houses — Shoham, Zaccur, and Ibri — but adding these gives seven, one too many. Ellicott's own solution is that the "Mahli" opening this verse is a mistaken repetition of the Mahli of v.21, an ancient scribe's error; strike it as spurious, and six clean Merarite clans remain, completing the twenty-four.

Keil and Delitzsch reach the opposite verdict from the same evidence. Where Bertheau had argued that a third son of Merari dropped out of this chapter, they judge rather that the extra material at 1 Chronicles 24:26 and the verse following are themselves arbitrary interpolations, not an authentic branch omitted here. So a genuine debate stands between two grammatical-historical readings: Ellicott holds that this chapter has suffered by a duplicated name and that Jaaziah's house in the next chapter is original, while Keil and Delitzsch hold that the next chapter has been swollen by interpolation and that this chapter's list, bar the stray Mahli, is sound.

Ellicott himself grants how delicate the ground is — the ancient versions all carry the present text of v.21 through v.23, so any omission must be very old, and elsewhere in Scripture only Mahli and Mushi are named as Merari's sons — yet concludes that it is easier to suppose a name accidentally doubled here than a whole family invented there. The witnesses leave the reader with the count honestly unresolved, but with the goal clear: however the Merarite houses are reckoned, they must come to six, that the four-and-twenty courses of the service may answer exactly to the four-and-twenty thousand who were to keep it.
24:
The long roster is sealed with a subscription — these were the sons of Levi after the house of their fathers... from the age of twenty years and upward — and the witnesses fasten at once on the number that clashes with the chapter's opening. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott takes the verse as the formal close of the list, the entry as it stood in David's muster-rolls, and confronts the difficulty squarely: v.3 had numbered the Levites "from thirty years old and upward," but here the age is twenty. Some, he reports, would banish the discrepancy by supposing "thirty" an ancient error of transcription; others imagine the Chronicler simply set down two divergent statements as he found them in his sources; while the older commentators held that David numbered the Levites twice — first, as the law required, from thirty (v.3), and again toward the close of his reign from twenty (v.27), because he saw the duties had grown lighter and might be borne by younger men. Ellicott notes too that twenty was the military age, the age of full strength, reckoned "from twenty years old and upward" at Numbers 1:3.

The reformed John Gill and the wesleyan-arminian voices take the road of the two numberings. Gill reads this as the new reckoning David ordered. Adam Clarke states it flatly: this is a different ordinance from that of v.3 — at first from thirty, now from twenty — David's last orders (v.27), the Levites to begin earlier and continue later, since with the ark now fixed and their numbers great there was no more heavy drudgery. John Wesley harmonizes both figures together: the Levites were numbered from twenty, when they were prepared, instructed, and by degrees exercised in their work, and from thirty, when they entered its full discharge — the earlier age now allowed because their task was eased of the old burden of carriage and the multiplied people needed more hands.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry gives the change its fullest reasons and its authority. David appointed the Levites numbered from twenty by direction from God, confirming it by his last words (v.27) as a thing to be done for ever, "yet not he, but the Lord." Henry offers three grounds: that the young Levites, idle till twenty-five under the old rule, were better set to work and brought under discipline at twenty; that the heavy carriage of the tabernacle, for which full strength had been required, was now at an end since God had given his people rest; and that the people being multiplied and their resort to Jerusalem greater, more hands were needed, that every Israelite bringing an offering might find a Levite ready to assist him. Keil and Delitzsch confirm the wording of the subscription, reading it of those counted "by number of names, by their polls," performing the work of the house of the LORD from twenty years old. The witnesses thus turn the bare clash of numbers into the hinge of the chapter's argument: the age was lowered because the work had changed, and the work had changed because the ark had come to rest.
25:
David's own reasoning is now given, and the witnesses hear in it the theological ground of the whole reorganization. For David said, The LORD God of Israel hath given rest unto his people, that they may dwell in Jerusalem for ever. The reformed John Gill takes the verse as the express reason David gave for ordering a new account of the Levites — the rest God had granted made the old rule of service obsolete. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott unfolds what that rest meant: the people no longer wander from pasture to pasture in the wilderness, nor are they any more oppressed by foreign tyrants as in the days of the judges; and he would render the second clause, "and He, the LORD, hath settled in Jerusalem for ever," so that it is God himself who has come to dwell in the city he has chosen. He recalls the LORD's own word that he had "gone from tent to tent" (1 Chronicles 17:5), and finds here the counterpart — Jehovah has now chosen Zion to be his eternal dwelling-place (Psalms 132:13).

Keil and Delitzsch join the verse to those that follow as one connected utterance of David: the LORD God of Israel has given rest to his people that they may dwell in Jerusalem for ever, and to the Levites also, who shall therefore no more carry the tabernacle or its vessels. The rest of the nation and the rest of the Levites are, on this reading, two sides of one gift; because God has settled his people and himself in the city, the ministering tribe is released from its ancient task of carriage and set to a new and settled service.

The witnesses converge on the great turn the verse records. The whole history of Israel's worship had been a history of movement — the tabernacle borne from Sinai through the wilderness, from Shiloh to Nob to Gibeon — and the Levites' oldest office was to carry it. Now that movement is over. God has given rest; he has chosen Jerusalem for his everlasting house; and David, reading the change aright, reorders the tribe of Levi to match it. The lowering of the age at which they served, which the witnesses debated at v.24, is here grounded in the deepest of reasons: not administrative convenience merely, but the fact that the God who once journeyed with his people in a tent has come home to Zion, and means to stay. The service must be refashioned because the LORD himself has settled, and his ministers must settle with him.
26:
The gift of rest is now applied directly to the Levites — and also unto the Levites; they shall no more carry the tabernacle, nor any vessels of it for the service thereof — and the witnesses read it as the release of the tribe from its most ancient labour. The reformed John Gill takes the words plainly as spoken with respect to the Levites: the rest God had given reaches them too. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott renders the clause "and the Levites also have not now to carry the dwelling and all its vessels for its service," as they had been bound to do in the wanderings of Israel through the desert. The sacred dwelling, he observes, the mishkan, had long stood fixed at Gibeon; and the Levites' charge was so much the lighter for it, since in the olden time they had not only to carry the tent and its furniture from place to place but also to guard the whole against the attacks of marauders. From this, he draws the inference the chapter keeps pressing: the duties having grown so much less arduous, they might well be undertaken at an earlier age than the ancient custom had allowed.

The witnesses do not divide; the verse is the plain premise of David's reform, and their comment only unfolds its force. The Levite's oldest identity was that of a bearer — the shoulder set under the ark and the boards, the tribe that moved the house of God through every stage of the wilderness march and every later resting-place. That whole vocation is here declared at an end. The tabernacle will move no more; the God who dwelt in it has chosen a settled house; and the men who carried the one are freed for the service of the other.

Ellicott's picture of the earlier Levites — carriers and armed guardians of a portable sanctuary always exposed to plunder — throws into relief how great the change is. Where once strength and vigilance for the road were required, now a younger man may serve, for the burden is gone. The verse thus supplies, in a single clause, the concrete reason behind the lowered age: not that the Levites are needed less, but that the heaviest part of what they were needed for has passed away with the resting of the ark, and a new and quieter kind of service has taken the place of the old labour of the march.
27:
A brief parenthesis fixes the authority for the change — for by the last words of David the Levites were numbered from twenty years old and above — and the witnesses weigh both its sense and its place in the passage. The reformed John Gill reads "the last words of David" as the last orders he gave before his death, the final ordinance by which the age of Levitical service was lowered. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott agrees that the phrase means "owing to his last commands," comparing the Vulgate's rendering, and takes the verse to record that David, toward the end of his reign, instituted a census of the Levites from twenty years old instead of thirty. But he notes a second possible sense: some would render it "in the Last Words," that is, the last records, of David, as though the Chronicler were referring the reader to some written work in which this special numbering was set down — a reference he compares with the citation of the histories of Samuel, Nathan, and Gad at 1 Chronicles 29:29.

Ellicott further observes the verse's function in its context. It is a parenthetic remark of the Chronicler himself, breaking into the speech of David begun at v.25 — the explanation of why the census now ran from twenty — which is then resumed at v.28 and carried through to the end of the chapter. So the compiler steps in for a sentence to note, as if in his own voice, the royal authority behind the lowered age, before letting David's words go on.

The witnesses agree on the substance whichever way the phrase is turned: whether by David's last commands or according to a record of his last words, the numbering of the Levites from twenty rests on the king's own final authority. Gill's plain reading and Ellicott's alternatives meet in that one point, which is the whole burden of the verse — that the change was no innovation of a later age but David's deliberate, dying ordinance. The Chronicler, careful as ever to ground the arrangements of the temple service in the authority of the king who planned the house, pauses here to stamp the lowered age with David's last word, and then returns to the reasons and the duties that word set in order.
28:
With David's speech resumed, the Levites' new charge is defined — because their office was to wait on the sons of Aaron for the service of the house of the LORD, in the courts, and in the chambers, and in the purifying of all holy things — and the witnesses set out the settled, subordinate ministry that replaced the old work of carriage. The reformed John Gill states the heart of it: their office was to wait on the sons of Aaron, that is, on the priests. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott renders the opening "for their appointment, or station, is at the side of the sons of Aaron," the Levites no longer bearing the tabernacle and its vessels but ministering, in subordination to the priesthood, in a permanent sanctuary. The "courts" he takes as the temple courts, and the "chambers" as the cells built around them, in which stores and treasures were kept and where priests and Levites lodged (1 Chronicles 9:26); the "purifying of all holy things" (2 Chronicles 30:19) meant the cleansing of the sacred vessels and of the sanctuary itself.

The wesleyan-arminian voices fill in the particulars. Adam Clarke glosses the purifying as keeping all the vessels and utensils of the sacred service clean and neat; John Wesley widens "holy things" to the holy places, garments, vessels, and sacrifices, all to be washed and cleansed from any defilement that might cleave to them. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry, surveying the Levites' work, casts their service as an honourable drudgery: they were to wait on the sons of Aaron, to do the lower work of the house of God — if any work for God may be called drudgery — keeping the courts and chambers clean, setting things in their places, and having them ready against the hour of need, that the priests might find everything to hand.

The witnesses converge on the shape of the reform this verse completes. The tribe that once carried the house of God is now stationed within it, ranged "at the side of" the priests as their appointed helpers, charged with its courts, its stores, its purity. Ellicott sees in the language the mark of a permanent institution, and Henry finds in the very lowliness of the tasks a dignity, since they were done for God and about his house. The old vocation of the road has become the new vocation of the sanctuary, and the Levite's greatness is now to serve the priest and keep the holy things clean and ready in a house that will not move.
29:
The specific tasks of the assisting Levites are now itemized — both for the shewbread, and for the fine flour for meat offering, and for the unleavened cakes... and for all manner of measure and size — and the witnesses divide the catalogue between the kitchen of the sanctuary and its standards of weight. The reformed John Gill takes the showbread as their charge to prepare and make ready each week, that the priests might set it fresh upon the table as they removed the old. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott renders the run of clauses as "over the showbread, and over the fine flour," the Levitical assistants seeing to the preparation of all the things here named: that which is "baked in the pan" (Leviticus 2:5), and that which the English calls "fried" but which he reads rather as "soaked in oil," a kind of cake (Leviticus 7:12).

On the closing phrase, "all manner of measure and size," the witnesses gather to a single striking point. Ellicott explains that the flour, wine, and oil which were the complements of every sacrifice were measured by the Levites in standard vessels of which they had the keeping, the proportions being fixed for each offering (Exodus 29:40); "measure" is a rare word for capacity, "size" a word for length. Adam Clarke enlarges it into the sanctuary's role as the nation's bureau of standards: the standards of all weights and measures were kept at the sanctuary, and by those there deposited all the weights and measures of the land were to be tried (Exodus 30:13). John Wesley says the same — these were all the measures used in sacred or civil things, whose public standards were kept in the temple, so that the care of keeping them inviolable and producing them when appealed to fell to the priests, and under them to the Levites.

Keil and Delitzsch, taking the verse with those around it, note how the list embraces every kind of preparation for the offerings, from the showbread to the baked and the fried, all the business of the house short of the strictly priestly acts. The witnesses converge on a double dignity in the Levites' humble work: they were at once the bakers and stewards of the sanctuary's provisions and the guardians of the nation's honest measure. The same men who prepared the fine flour and the cakes kept the true weights by which all Israel bought and sold — a quiet reminder that the house of God was the fountain not only of worship but of justice in the market, and that even the measuring-vessels of the land were held sacred in the keeping of the ministering tribe.
30:
The service of song is set in its daily rhythm — and to stand every morning to thank and praise the LORD, and likewise at even — and the witnesses agree on both its times and its performers. The reformed John Gill assigns it to a distinct body: the office of others among the Levites was to sing the praises of God, both with the voice and with instruments of music, at the hour of the morning sacrifice. The wesleyan-arminian voices set it at the two great moments of the day. Adam Clarke notes that at the offering of the morning and evening sacrifice they sounded their musical instruments and sang praises to God; John Wesley names the same two solemn times of sacrifice, the work attended with public prayer and thanksgiving.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott makes a point of who these singers were and were not. This standing to thank and praise, he insists, refers to the special function of the four thousand musicians of v.5 (compare 1 Chronicles 16:4), and not to the Levites who assisted at the altar; for those who slew and flayed the victims could hardly at the same time have taken part in the service of song. The choir was a separate company, set apart to its own ministry of praise, morning and evening, while other hands did the bloody work of the sacrifice.

The witnesses converge on the ordered beauty of the arrangement. Twice each day, as the lamb was offered at dawn and at dusk, a trained body of Levites stood to lift the thanksgiving of Israel in word and instrument, their praise framing the sacrifice as its appointed accompaniment. Gill's note that they sang "both vocally and with instruments," Clarke's and Wesley's fixing of the hours, and Ellicott's careful separation of the singers from the slaughterers together yield a picture of worship divided by gift and function: some to prepare and some to praise, the house of God served at once by labouring hands and by lifted voices. The verse thus completes the fourfold ministry the chapter has been unfolding — servers, magistrates, gatekeepers, and now, standing morning and evening before the LORD, the singers whose whole office was to give him thanks.
31:
The Levites' part in the great public offerings is defined — and to offer all burnt sacrifices unto the LORD in the sabbaths, in the new moons, and on the set feasts, by number, according to the order commanded unto them, continually before the LORD — and the witnesses take pains to say exactly what a Levite's "offering" could mean. The reformed John Gill is careful that the actual offering was the priests' work and not the Levites'; the sense, he says with the Targum and Kimchi, is that the Levites sang the praises of God whenever burnt offerings were presented, and that some of them besides helped the priests in slaying the sacrifices and bringing them to the altar. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott reads the phrase the same way: "over all offering of burnt offerings," the Levites selecting and preparing the victims, the priests offering them when ready upon the altar. The offering was to be done "by number," that is, according to the several numbers the law prescribed for each occasion (Numbers 28:1), and "according to the order concerning them," the fixed rule for the sacrifices; "continually" he marks as the technical term for the burnt offerings that recurred at their stated times, the lamb of morning and evening (Numbers 28:6).

Keil and Delitzsch describe the Levites' share in fuller detail: it was their duty to procure the necessary number of beasts for sacrifice, to see to their suitableness, and to slaughter and skin them, so that the offering might be presented on the sabbaths, the new moons, and the feasts, in the number the law required, continually before Jahve. The word "continually," they add, belongs to the burnt offerings, which are so called because they must always be offered anew on the appointed days.

The witnesses do not finally divide, though they distribute the labour a little differently — Gill leaning on the Levites' song and their aid in slaying, Ellicott and Keil and Delitzsch on their procuring, preparing, and skinning of the victims. What they hold in common is the careful subordination the verse enshrines: the Levites do everything up to the altar, and the priests alone do the act upon it. The great calendar of Israel's worship — sabbath and new moon and feast, and the unbroken daily offering — is served by a whole graduated ministry, the Levites furnishing and readying, the priests presenting, and all of it done "by number" and "according to the order commanded," that nothing in the house of God might be left to improvisation, but every sacrifice offered exactly as the law had fixed.
32:
The chapter closes by gathering the Levites' whole charge into three clauses — that they should keep the charge of the tabernacle of the congregation, and the charge of the holy place, and the charge of the sons of Aaron their brethren, in the service of the house of the LORD — and the witnesses read it as the summary of everything the preceding verses have unfolded. The reformed John Gill fastens on the first clause as the porters' business: to keep the charge of the tabernacle meant to see that no unclean person entered it and that none of its vessels were carried out. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott sets out all three heads together — the charge of the tent of meeting, of the holy place with its sacred things, and of the sons of Aaron, that is, all that the priests committed to them and required of them as their appointed assistants — noting that the word rendered "charge" means literally a keeping, a guard or watch, and that the whole is plainly based on the law of the Levites at Numbers 18:3. Keil and Delitzsch sum the same three duties — the charge of the tabernacle, of all the sacred things of the worship, and of whatever the priests entrusted to them — and refer them likewise to the fuller ordinance of Numbers 18:3.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke presses the last clause into its plainest sense: "the charge of the sons of Aaron" means that, since it was the priests' business to kill, flay, dress, and offer the victims but the priests were few, they employed the Levites to flay the animals; the Levites were properly the servants of the priests, engaged in the more servile part of the divine worship. So the exalted tribe of Levi ends the chapter defined by its subordination, the hands that keep the doors, guard the holy things, and do for the priests what the priests have not hands enough to do themselves.

Ellicott adds a closing caution against a common charge. It is hardly fair, he says against Reuss, to claim that the writer confounded the tabernacle of David with the temple of Solomon; for the Chronicler had clearly distinguished two sacred tents, the one for the ark on Zion and the ancient sanctuary at Gibeon, and if David here speaks of "courts" and "chambers" it only shows that he meant his assignment of the Levites' duties to be permanent, reaching on into the settled house to come. The great historian Ewald, he notes, believed this whole section an authentic extract from an early record of Solomon's reign. The witnesses close the chapter, then, on a double note: the Levites bound for ever to the keeping of God's house and the service of his priests, and the arrangement itself vouched as no late invention but the genuine, deliberate ordering of David in his last days.
